assumptions: Hungary's education system operates under a centralized two-tier expert-committee structure for SNI/BTMN classification separate from medical diagnosis, governed by EMMI decree 15/2013
Parental consent is the default procedural requirement for initiating SEN assessment, with administrative override available only when child welfare is at risk
Per-pupil financing mechanisms tie school funding to SNI classification, creating structural incentives that may influence identification practices
The distinction between the SNI and BTMN categories carries significant legal consequences regarding assessment exemptions, placement, and resource allocation

findings.claim: The recorded SNI population in Hungary has grown continuously since at least 2012/13, with documented acceleration from 2021/22 onwards, rising from 107,421 to 112,007 students across kindergarten and primary/secondary institutions in the most recent school year.
limitations: Absolute totals differ across sources (63k for primary-school-only vs. ~105–112k for all levels) due to scope and definition inconsistency across reporting systems
No single agreed national figure exists
Causal drivers of the growth remain contested across sources
findings.source_title: Hungarian Central Statistical Office (KSH) data compiled by Telex and recent KSH preliminary figures

uncertainties.question: To what extent is the acceleration of SNI identification since 2021/22 attributable to the 2017–18 legal tightening of BTMN exemptions versus other factors such as diagnostic-practice changes, COVID-era effects, environmental factors, or genuine prevalence increases?
uncertainties.reduction_path: Conduct a controlled comparative analysis of identification patterns before and after the September 2018 implementation, controlling for regional variation, teacher-training changes, and concurrent policy shifts; compare with epidemiological data on actual prevalence of learning/attention/behavioral disorders from independent sources

uncertainties.question: Do the per-pupil financing incentives embedded in task-based funding (counting SNI pupils as two or three pupils for subsidy calculation) materially drive over-identification at the school level, and to what magnitude?
uncertainties.reduction_path: Analyze school-level identification decisions against institutional budget pressures and staffing outcomes; conduct qualitative interviews with placement committee members and school administrators on decision-making factors; compare identification rates across schools with differing financial health and specialist workforce capacity

uncertainties.question: What is the extent of ongoing discriminatory misclassification of Roma children in the SEN assessment system, and why has Hungary not closed the Horváth and Kiss enforcement case despite over a decade of Committee of Ministers monitoring?
uncertainties.reduction_path: Obtain ethnically-disaggregated data on identification rates by Roma status, disability category, and placement outcomes; conduct independent audit of expert-committee decision-making protocols against non-discrimination safeguards; review government enforcement documentation and remedial measures submitted to the Committee of Ministers

uncertainties.question: How do regional disparities in specialist workforce availability (e.g., zero special education teachers in some northeastern districts) interact with SNI identification patterns, and does this create a two-tier access problem?
uncertainties.reduction_path: Map specialist teacher employment by district and correlate against identification rates and actual service delivery capacity; interview parents and educators in low-capacity regions on whether identification occurs without corresponding provision; analyze whether informal/private reimbursement patterns cluster in specific geographies
